title: American Economy
desc: From gold rush to Silicon Valley — how America became an economic superpower

lessons.title: The Industrial Revolution in America
lessons.content:
From Farm to Factory

In 1800, 90% of Americans were farmers. By 1900, America was the world's largest industrial economy. What happened?

Key factors: railroads connected the continent, immigration provided labor, innovation (Edison, Bell, Carnegie) created new industries, and property rights protected inventors and investors.

The Transcontinental Railroad (1869) was a game-changer: it connected East Coast markets to West Coast resources, creating the largest free-trade zone in the world.

quiz.q: What percentage of Americans were farmers in 1800?
quiz.exp: About 90% of Americans were farmers in 1800. Industrialization, railroads, and urbanization transformed the economy so dramatically that by 1900, manufacturing output exceeded agriculture.

lessons.title: Robber Barons or Captains of Industry?
lessons.sub: Carnegie, Rockefeller, and the Gilded Age
lessons.content:
Giants of American Capitalism

Andrew Carnegie (steel), John D. Rockefeller (oil), and J.P. Morgan (banking) built empires that transformed America. Critics called them "robber barons." Supporters called them "captains of industry."

Carnegie reduced steel prices by 80%, making bridges, railroads, and skyscrapers affordable. Rockefeller cut kerosene prices by 90%, bringing light to millions. Both later gave away most of their fortunes to charity.

The debate continues: were they exploitative monopolists or visionary entrepreneurs? Probably both. Their innovations created immense wealth, but their labor practices were often brutal.

quiz.q: What did Carnegie do to steel prices?
quiz.exp: Carnegie's innovations in steel production reduced prices by about 80%. This made construction cheaper and enabled America's infrastructure boom — bridges, railroads, skyscrapers.

lessons.title: The Great Depression
lessons.content:
1929: The Crash

The stock market crashed on October 29, 1929 ("Black Tuesday"). What followed was the worst economic crisis in modern history: 25% unemployment, bank failures, breadlines.

Causes: Federal Reserve contracted money supply (Milton Friedman's analysis), Smoot-Hawley Tariff killed international trade, and bank runs destroyed savings.

The lesson: government policy can make crises worse. The Fed's tight money policy and protectionist tariffs turned a recession into a catastrophe. Free-market economists argue the Depression lasted so long because of intervention, not despite it.

quiz.q: According to Milton Friedman, what worsened the Great Depression?
o: The Federal Reserve contracting the money supply
quiz.exp: Friedman showed that the Federal Reserve dramatically contracted the money supply (by ~1/3) during the Depression, turning a normal recession into a catastrophe. This is now mainstream economic consensus.

lessons.title: World War II Economy
lessons.content:
War and Prosperity

WWII ended the Depression — not through government spending per se, but through full mobilization of resources. Factories that made cars now made tanks. Unemployment went from 25% to nearly 0%.

America produced: 300,000 aircraft, 86,000 tanks, 2.7 million machine guns, and 6,500 ships in just 4 years. This industrial might won the war.

After the war, America had 50% of global GDP. Europe and Japan were in ruins. This dominance launched the "American Century" of economic leadership.

quiz.q: What percentage of global GDP did America have after WWII?
quiz.exp: After WWII, America had roughly 50% of global GDP because it was the only major industrial nation not physically destroyed by the war. This economic dominance shaped the global order for decades.

lessons.title: The Post-War Boom
lessons.sub: Suburbs, highways, and the American Dream
lessons.content:
1945-1970: The Golden Age

The post-war era was America's economic golden age: rising wages, growing middle class, suburban expansion, and the Interstate Highway System.

Key drivers: GI Bill (education for veterans), Bretton Woods system (stable currencies), and technological innovation (TV, jet travel, computers).

A single-income family could buy a house, a car, and send kids to college. Real wages grew 2-3% per year. This era shaped the "American Dream" ideal.

o: Education and housing benefits for veterans
A bill to increase military spending
quiz.exp: The GI Bill (1944) gave returning veterans education funding, low-cost mortgages, and business loans. It created America's educated middle class and fueled the post-war economic boom.

lessons.title: Silicon Valley
lessons.sub: How a valley became the world's innovation capital
lessons.content:
From Garages to Global Empires

Silicon Valley (San Francisco Bay Area) became the world's tech capital through a unique combination: Stanford University's research, military contracts (Cold War), venture capital, and a culture that celebrated risk-taking and failure.

Key milestones: HP (1939, in a garage), Intel (1968, the microchip), Apple (1976), Google (1998), Facebook (2004). Each generation built on the last.

The Valley's secret sauce: people can fail and try again. Bankruptcy isn't shameful — it's a learning experience. This tolerance for risk doesn't exist in most countries.

o: A culture that tolerates failure and rewards risk-taking
quiz.exp: Silicon Valley's key ingredient is cultural: failure is not stigmatized but seen as learning. Combined with top universities, venture capital, and immigrant talent, this creates unmatched innovation.

lessons.title: The Dollar: World Reserve Currency
lessons.sub: Why the world uses American money
lessons.content:
The Almighty Dollar

The U.S. dollar is the world's reserve currency: 60% of global foreign exchange reserves, 88% of international transactions, and oil is priced in dollars ("petrodollar").

This gives America an "exorbitant privilege" — it can borrow cheaply, print money for imports, and sanction any country by cutting it off from the dollar system.

The dollar became dominant after Bretton Woods (1944) when it was pegged to gold. Nixon ended the gold standard in 1971, but dollar dominance continued due to trust in U.S. institutions and markets.

quiz.q: What percentage of international transactions use the U.S. dollar?
quiz.exp: About 88% of international transactions use the dollar. This gives America enormous economic power — the ability to borrow cheaply, influence global trade, and use financial sanctions.

lessons.title: American Entrepreneurship
lessons.content:
Why America Breeds Entrepreneurs

America produces more billion-dollar startups ("unicorns") than any other country. Why?

  • Easy to start a business — minimal bureaucracy
  • Venture capital — investors fund risky ideas
  • Bankruptcy laws — you can fail and restart
  • Immigration — 55% of unicorn founders are immigrants
  • Culture — entrepreneurship is admired, not suspicious

Compare: starting a business in Brazil takes 150 days. In America: 6 days. This difference matters enormously.

quiz.q: What percentage of American unicorn founders are immigrants?
quiz.exp: About 55% of America's billion-dollar startups (unicorns) were founded by immigrants. This includes Google (Sergey Brin, Russia), Tesla (Elon Musk, South Africa), and WhatsApp (Jan Koum, Ukraine).

lessons.title: Inequality in America
lessons.content:
The Land of Opportunity?

The top 1% of Americans own more wealth than the bottom 50% combined. CEO pay is 350x the average worker (in 1965 it was 21x).

Two perspectives:
Progressive: inequality is unjust, needs redistribution.
Free-market: inequality of outcomes is natural; what matters is equality of opportunity.

The data: social mobility in America has declined. A child born poor today has a 7.5% chance of reaching the top quintile — lower than in Denmark or Canada. The American Dream is harder to achieve than before.

quiz.q: What has happened to social mobility in America?
quiz.exp: Social mobility in America has declined over decades. A child born in the bottom 20% today has lower chances of reaching the top 20% than in several other developed nations.

lessons.title: The Future of the American Economy
lessons.content:
What's Next?

America faces challenges: $34 trillion national debt, aging infrastructure, healthcare costs, and competition from China.

But strengths remain: world's best universities, deepest capital markets, innovation culture, rule of law, and immigration (talent from everywhere).

Key question: can America maintain the institutions that made it great? Property rights, limited government, free speech, and market competition. Nations decline when they abandon the principles that made them successful.

quiz.q: What is America's biggest economic strength going forward?
quiz.exp: America's greatest economic asset is its innovation ecosystem: top universities, venture capital, tolerance for risk, rule of law, and ability to attract global talent. These institutions drive long-term prosperity.
